ideas.title: Behavioural Cybersecurity Training
ideas.summary: Replace compliance checkboxes with behavioural science — simulations, gamified learning, and micro-moments that build lasting security habits.
ideas.intent: Understand the current enterprise cybersecurity training landscape, identify its shortcomings, and investigate how behavioural science and AI can improve human resilience against social engineering attacks.
ideas.lastInsight: Compliance training teaches knowledge. But risky behaviour happens in half a second of habit. Knowledge doesn't reach that moment.

connections.relation: Both challenge a reflex — the engineer's urge to build, and the click that happens before the brain catches up. The answer in both cases is pause, not speed.
Both believe that better outcomes come from gentle, well-timed cues — not more dashboards, not more training videos, not more data to log.

ideas.title: Passive Life Analytics
ideas.summary: Turn the behavioural data your devices already collect into gentle, useful insight — without asking you to track one more thing.
ideas.intent: Research existing behavioural datasets available on personal devices and explore how AI can transform passive signals into meaningful coaching without increasing user effort.
ideas.lastInsight: The data already exists — Screen Time, calendars, location history, development tool activity. The opportunity is AI synthesis, not new collection.

connections.relation: Both resist the impulse to act immediately — one delays the build, the other delays the tracking. The discipline is identical: understand what's already there before adding more.
Both believe that better outcomes come from gentle, well-timed cues — not more dashboards, not more training videos, not more data to log.

ideas.title: Thinking Before Building
ideas.summary: A deliberate practice of staying in the problem space longer — separating product thinking from engineering thinking before any architecture is drawn.
ideas.intent: Explore frameworks and mental models that help founders and engineers stay in the problem space longer before converging on implementation.
ideas.lastInsight: The impulse to design systems is so strong that it crowds out the harder work of understanding what the system is actually for.

connections.relation: Both resist the impulse to act immediately — one delays the build, the other delays the tracking. The discipline is identical: understand what's already there before adding more.
Both challenge a reflex — the engineer's urge to build, and the click that happens before the brain catches up. The answer in both cases is pause, not speed.
